title: The Aftermath of Gupta
bullets: The Gupta Empire fragmented gradually over a century after Hephthalite Hun invasions drained the treasury — provincial governors stopped sending tribute, regional kings declared independence, and by 550 the imperial fiction had stopped being worth maintaining.
Yashodharman of Malwa defeated the Hun king Mihirakula at the Battle of Sondani around 528, ending the Hun threat to north India and making the regional patchwork of the next century possible.

Six regional dynasties divided the post-Gupta subcontinent — the Maukharis at Kannauj, the Pushyabhutis at Thanesar, the Maitrakas at Vallabhi, the Chalukyas of Vatapi, the Pallavas of Kanchipuram, and a handful of Bengal successor kingdoms.

Almost every famous Indian temple — Brihadeeswara, Khajuraho, Kailasa at Ellora, Konark, Belur, Halebidu — was built during the medieval period; the temple-as-civic-institution becomes the central organizing structure of Indian society in these centuries.

The slow disappearance of Buddhism from north India was already underway during this period — Hindu philosophical absorption, royal patronage shifts, and devotional competition would all play roles before the final Turkic destruction around 1200.

The structural problem was that no Indian dynasty managed to reconsolidate after the Hun threat ended, and the political fragmentation that followed lasted six and a half centuries until the Delhi Sultanate.

The cross-civ comparison is the post-Roman west — Western Europe stayed politically fragmented after 476 CE for a thousand years until the early-modern nation-states coalesced, and India was beginning a similar process at roughly the same scale.
subtitle: the long fragmentation that followed the Gupta collapse

title: Harsha and the Last North-Indian Empire
bullets: Harshavardhana of the Pushyabhuti dynasty took the throne at sixteen in 606 CE after his older brother was assassinated, then spent the next decade pursuing his enemy Shashanka of Bengal and consolidating control across the upper Ganges plain from his new capital at Kannauj.

Banabhatta, Harsha's court poet, composed the Harshacharita around 640 — the first surviving Sanskrit historical biography of an actual Indian king and a model for subsequent court literature across the medieval period.

The Chinese Buddhist monk Xuanzang spent fifteen years traveling India between 630 and 645, returning to Tang China with 657 Sanskrit manuscripts and a travelogue, the Records of the Western Regions, that remains the single most detailed eyewitness account of seventh-century Indian society.

Pulakeshin II of the Chalukyas of Vatapi decisively defeated Harsha at the Narmada River around 618 CE, fixing the political boundary between north and south India that would persist for the rest of Harsha's reign.
Harsha personally wrote three Sanskrit plays — the Ratnavali, Priyadarshika, and Nagananda — making him one of the only reigning Indian emperors who was also a credentialed literary author.

When Harsha died in 647 CE without an heir, his empire dissolved within months and a chaotic succession crisis culminated in a Tang Chinese diplomatic mission being attacked, a Chinese ambassador raising a Tibetan-Nepalese army, and the would-be successor being captured and shipped to China as a prisoner.
After Harsha, no one would unite north India for the next 879 years until Babur founded the Mughal Empire in 1526.
subtitle: the seventh-century reunification that died with its emperor

title: The South Rises
bullets: While the post-Gupta north fragmented, the Pallava king Mahendravarman I introduced rock-cut Hindu temple architecture in the Tamil south around 600 CE, and his son Narasimhavarman I sacked the Chalukya capital at Vatapi in 642, killing Pulakeshin II.

Under the Pallavas, the coastal site at Mahabalipuram was carved into the Pancha Rathas, the Descent of the Ganges (the largest open-air bas-relief in India), and the Shore Temple — UNESCO World Heritage and the laboratory where the Dravida south Indian temple style was worked out.

The Chalukya complex at Pattadakal in modern Karnataka shows ten major temples built in both the Nagara north-Indian and Dravida south-Indian styles side by side — the experimental laboratory where the hybrid Vesara Deccan style emerged.

The Rashtrakuta dynasty overthrew the Chalukyas in 753 and built the most powerful early-medieval Indian empire — the Arab geographer al-Masudi called the Rashtrakuta king one of the four great kings of the world alongside the Abbasid caliph, the Byzantine emperor, and the emperor of China.

The Rashtrakuta king Krishna I commissioned the Kailasa Temple at Ellora around 770 — a full-sized Shiva temple carved downward out of a single basalt cliff, removing roughly 200,000 tons of rock, the largest single monolithic structure in the world.
The Pandyas of Madurai controlled the Gulf of Mannar pearl fisheries famous since Roman times, while the Cheras of the Malabar coast grew wealthy on the spice trade with Arab and Persian merchants.

Tradition holds that the Cheraman Juma Mosque in Kerala was founded around 629 CE by a Chera king who converted to Islam through trade contact, possibly making it the oldest mosque in India and the foundational example of peaceful Indian-Muslim coexistence through commerce.
subtitle: Mahabalipuram, Ellora, and the southern flowering while the north fragmented

title: The Tripartite Struggle for Kannauj
bullets: Three regional powers — the Gurjara-Pratiharas of Rajasthan, the Palas of Bengal, and the Rashtrakutas of the Deccan — fought a three-way war for control of Kannauj for two and a half centuries; none of them ever held it long enough to translate dominance into a durable north Indian empire.

The Pratihara emperor Mihira Bhoja ruled for nearly fifty years from 836 to 885 and the Arab merchant-traveler Sulayman called his army the strongest cavalry force in India and his roads the safest for foreign traders crossing the subcontinent.

The Pala dynasty of Bengal was unusual in being formally Buddhist for most of its existence, sponsoring the great monastic universities at Nalanda, Vikramashila, and Odantapuri that kept Indian Buddhism intellectually alive into the eleventh and twelfth centuries.

The Pala emperor Dharmapala briefly captured Kannauj from the Pratiharas around 790 and installed his own client king on the throne — the high-water mark of Pala power and the moment the three-way Tripartite Struggle visibly began.

The Rashtrakuta king Govinda III defeated both the Pala emperor Dharmapala and the Pratihara emperor Nagabhata II in a single campaign around 800 CE, briefly capturing Kannauj before being forced to withdraw south to defend the Deccan.

Mahmud of Ghazni's sack of Kannauj in 1019 finally ended the city's role as the symbolic capital of north India, and after the sack the Pratihara king Rajyapala was killed by his own neighbor for fleeing without fighting.

The cumulative cost of two and a half centuries of three-way war was that all three competing dynasties had spent themselves, leaving the Rajput successor states fragmented and unable to coordinate when the Turkic invasions came.
subtitle: two and a half centuries of three-way war for one symbolic city

title: The Chola Maritime Empire
bullets: Vijayalaya seized Thanjavur around 848 CE and refounded the medieval Chola dynasty, claiming descent from the ancient Tamil Cholas of Sangam-era poetry; over two centuries his successors built south India's first true empire.

Rajaraja Chola I commissioned the first detailed land survey of the Tamil country, conquered Sri Lanka and the Maldives, standardized the Chola administrative system, and consecrated the Brihadeeswara Temple at Thanjavur in 1010 to mark his twenty-fifth jubilee.

The Brihadeeswara Temple's central tower rises 216 feet, was built from 130,000 tons of granite hauled from quarries thirty miles away, and was probably the tallest structure in India and possibly the tallest Hindu temple in the world when consecrated.

Rajendra Chola I marched a land army across north India to the Ganges around 1019, earning the title Gangaikonda Chola — Chola who took the Ganges — and founded a new capital city named Gangaikonda Cholapuram with a second great temple.

Rajendra's naval expedition crossed roughly 1,200 nautical miles of open ocean to attack the Srivijayan thalassocracy in modern Sumatra around 1025, capturing fourteen tributary cities including Palembang and the Srivijayan king himself.

The Chola raid on Srivijaya is the only confirmed pre-modern Indian transoceanic military expedition — the closest comparison is Athens' disastrous Sicilian Expedition of 415 BCE, except the Cholas won.

Tamil merchant guilds called the Ainurruvar — the Five Hundred — operated trading houses across maritime Southeast Asia and the South China Sea, the most sophisticated medieval merchant network east of the Persian Gulf.
Chola bronze sculpture using the lost-wax process produced the iconic Nataraja figures of Shiva as Lord of the Dance that now hang in major museums on every continent.
subtitle: Brihadeeswara, the Ganges expedition, and a fleet that crossed the Bay of Bengal

title: Bhakti and the Temple Kingdoms
bullets: The Bhakti devotional movement began in the Tamil south in the seventh century with two parallel groups of poet-saints — the twelve Alvars devoted to Vishnu and the sixty-three Nayanars devoted to Shiva — composing thousands of vernacular Tamil hymns that broke the Sanskrit-priestly monopoly on serious religious literature.

Andal, the only female Alvar, composed the Tiruppavai and Nachiar Tirumozhi as passionate Tamil love poems addressed to Vishnu in the voice of a young woman in love with the deity; her hymns are still sung in south Indian temples every December.

The Bhakti movement was an early egalitarian challenge to caste-bound religious hierarchy — several of the most important poet-saints came from low castes, including Tiruppan Alvar from the bardic Panar caste and the non-Brahmin sage Tirumular.

The Chandela kings built eighty-five temples at Khajuraho in central India between 950 and 1050, of which about twenty-five survive — the canonical examples of the Nagara north Indian temple style with its mountain-like aggregate shikhara towers.

The famously erotic sculptures at Khajuraho occupy roughly 10% of the external sculptural program and were carved openly with full royal sponsorship — what subsequent observers found scandalous, the medieval Chandela patrons regarded as auspicious depictions of religiously sanctioned human experience.

The Eastern Ganga king Narasimhadeva I built the Sun Temple at Konark around 1250 as a colossal stone chariot of the sun god — twelve carved wheels for the months, seven horses for the days of the week, sanctum that originally rose perhaps 230 feet before its tower collapsed.

The Hoysala temples at Belur, Halebidu, and Somnathpur in southern Karnataka covered every external surface with thousands of densely sculpted figures in soft soapstone, producing the most ornately decorated medieval Indian buildings.

The Chaulukya minister Vimal Shah built the first of the marble-clad Dilwara temples on Mount Abu in 1031 — the most exquisitely refined Jain temple architecture anywhere, paid for by the merchant-Jain communities of medieval Gujarat.
subtitle: Khajuraho, Konark, the Hoysala temples, and the rise of personal devotion

title: Vedanta, Jainism, and the Slow Disappearance of Buddhism
bullets: Adi Shankara systematized Advaita Vedanta in the early ninth century — the radical monist position that ultimate reality and the individual self are not two but one, and that the apparent multiplicity of the world is a cognitive illusion arising from ignorance.

Shankara died around age thirty-two and founded four monastic centers at the four geographic corners of India — Sringeri, Dvaraka, Jagannath Puri, and Jyotirmath — that continue to operate today and are headed by lineal successors called Shankaracharyas.

Ramanuja built the rival Vishishtadvaita school in the late eleventh century, holding that individual souls and God are united but not identical and that personal devotion to a personal deity is the highest spiritual path — providing the theological foundation for the Vaishnava devotional tradition that flourishes across south India today.

Madhvacharya founded Dvaita Vedanta in the late thirteenth century with a strictly dualist position — God and souls are eternally distinct realities — and was the only major Indian philosopher to argue that some souls are eternally damned, a position uncomfortably close to certain Christian theological strands.

The Western Ganga minister Chamundaraya commissioned the colossal 57-foot Gomateshwara statue of the Jain saint Bahubali at Shravanabelagola in 981 — the tallest free-standing monolithic statue in India, ritually bathed once every twelve years.

Bhaskara II at the Ujjain observatory worked out principles of differential calculus several centuries before Newton and Leibniz, computed the length of the sidereal year to within minutes of the modern value, and wrote the Lilavati arithmetic textbook in elegant Sanskrit verse.

The Persian polymath al-Khwarizmi introduced the Indian decimal place-value numeral system to the Arab world around 825 CE, calling them Hindi numerals; Europeans later called them Arabic numerals because they got them from Arabic translations, forgetting the Indian origin.

Buddhism slowly disappeared from India between 500 and 1300 CE through a combination of royal patronage shifts, Hindu philosophical absorption, devotional competition, and finally the Turkic destruction of Nalanda and Vikramashila around 1200 — surviving everywhere it had been transplanted but vanishing from the land that invented it.
subtitle: Shankara, Ramanuja, Madhva, and the religion that disappeared from its birthplace

title: The Ghaznavid and Ghurid Invasions
bullets: Mahmud of Ghazni conducted seventeen extraction raids into northern India between 1001 and 1027, accumulating enormous portable wealth that funded his magnificent capital at Ghazni and the Persian poet Ferdowsi who wrote the Shahnameh under his patronage.

Mahmud's army sacked the great Shiva temple at Somnath on the Saurashtra coast in 1025, smashing the lingam and carrying off thousands of pounds of gold; Somnath has been rebuilt seven or eight times since and the trauma became a foundational image in subsequent Hindu memory.

Al-Biruni, the Khwarazmian polymath captured by Mahmud and brought to Ghazni as a prize, learned Sanskrit, traveled across northwestern India, and wrote the Tarikh al-Hind around 1030 — the most thoughtful outsider account of medieval Indian civilization from any source.

Muhammad of Ghor invaded the Indian plains a century and a half later and was defeated by Prithviraj Chauhan at the First Battle of Tarain in 1191 — the first major Hindu military victory over Turkic invaders in two centuries, and the last.

At the Second Battle of Tarain in 1192, Muhammad of Ghor used cavalry archers more flexibly, exhausted the Rajput formations, captured Prithviraj Chauhan, and opened the road to Delhi for the Turkic conquest.

Muhammad's slave-general Bakhtiyar Khalji overran Bihar and Bengal around 1200–1205, destroying Nalanda, Vikramashila, and Odantapuri, killing the resident Buddhist monks and burning libraries that contained six centuries of accumulated Sanskrit philosophical literature.

Most surviving medieval Indian Buddhist philosophical texts exist today only because Tibetan monks had translated them into Tibetan in the centuries before Bakhtiyar Khalji's destruction; modern scholars are reconstructing major works from Tibetan back-translation.

Qutb al-Din Aibak, Muhammad of Ghor's Turkic slave-general, proclaimed himself sultan at Lahore in 1206 and founded the Mamluk dynasty — the first ruling house of the Delhi Sultanate, beginning seven centuries of Muslim-ruled imperial states in north India.

The same year — 1206 — Genghis Khan was proclaimed Great Khan of the Mongols at the kurultai on the Onon River, launching the Eurasian empire that would later produce the Mughal dynasty that united north India after the Sultanate.
subtitle: Mahmud, Tarain, the destruction of Nalanda, and the road to Delhi
